title: ADK-104: Tool Schema Design & Basic APIs
description: Design robust agent tool definitions using strict parameter schemas and type reflections to wrap weather and currency APIs.

highlights.title: JSON Schema Design
highlights.description: Construct declarative type schemas for custom parameters that the LLM consumes for tool selection.

highlights.title: Pydantic Typing
highlights.description: Use Pydantic models to assert validation bounds on inputs, catching parameter typos immediately.

highlights.title: API Integrations
highlights.description: Configure external request boundaries, managing HTTP status codes and payloads safely.

blocks.content: JSON Schema Descriptions
The LLM backend reads function descriptions and argument descriptions to decide which tool to call. If your descriptions are ambiguous, the LLM will hallucinate arguments or pick the wrong tool.
Schema Reflection
ADK translates language docstrings and types to a JSON Schema parameter declaration:

blocks.code:
@tool
def lookup_user_id(email: str) -> str:
    """
    Retrieves the unique UUID for an active database customer record.
    
    Args:
        email: The raw email address, e.g., 'user@example.com'.
    """
    return "usr_8f4a12b"

blocks.title: Docstring Formatting
blocks.content: ADK expects standard docstring structures (such as Google Style or Sphinx) to successfully map description fields to JSON arguments.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What is the main source of description information for tool arguments?
- A) Global metadata dictionaries
- B) Function docstrings and comments
- C) Local system configurations

Feedback: Option B is correct. Docstrings describe the tool's usage to the LLM.

modules.title: 2. Defining strict Pydantic structures for tool inputs

blocks.content: Pydantic Validation for Tool Inputs

Pydantic offers strict, runtime class validation. By using Pydantic models in tool signatures, you ensure parameters adhere to specific constraints (like string length, email format, or enum constants).
Pydantic Tool Integration

blocks.title: Validation Failures
blocks.content: If the LLM passes an invalid payload (e.g. invalid email structure), Pydantic triggers a ValidationError, which ADK returns to the LLM to auto-correct.
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What happens when a tool argument fails Pydantic validation?
- A) The host program crashes.
- B) The agent exits immediately.
- C) A validation exception returns to the LLM so it can attempt a corrected tool call.

Feedback: Option C is correct. ADK catches validation errors and allows the agent loop to self-heal.
Clean API Tool Design
Tools should do one thing and return structured data. Avoid returning raw HTML, giant nested blobs, or unformatted text logs to the LLM, as this wastes tokens.

items.title: Single Responsibility
items.content: One function should solve one exact query (e.g., look up order vs edit order).

items.title: Filtering Blobs
items.content: Discard metadata fields (like headers, tracing keys) before returning data to the agent.

items.title: Error Boundaries
items.content: Never throw uncaught generic exceptions; catch them and return error messages.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding

Question 1: Why should tool returns be compact and structured?
- A) It compiles the project faster.
- B) To minimize token consumption and guide clean LLM generation.
- C) It is required by the OAuth standard.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Keeping responses minimal saves token costs.
Handling Tool Exceptions
If a tool makes an HTTP request that returns a 500 error or throws a database connection error, it must handle the issue and provide semantic error descriptions.
Exception Safe Python Tool
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: How should a tool handle a failed HTTP request?
- A) Exit the process using sys.exit(1).
- B) Catch the error and return a JSON dictionary containing the error message.
- C) Throw a raw exception and let the program crash.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Returning errors as standard tool outputs allows the agent to handle the failure gracefully.

modules.title: 5. Lab: Writing a structured weather and currency conversion tool

blocks.content: Lab: Structured Weather & Currency Tools
In this lab, you will author two custom tools in a python file, registering them via `agent.yaml`, and ensuring their parameters utilize clean, documented schemas.
Step 1: Write the Tool Source Code
Step 2: Register in agent.yaml
Check Your Understanding

Question 1: What would happen if the agent tried to convert USD to JPY using the convert_currency tool above?
- A) The host environment crashes.
- B) The tool returns a dictionary with 'success: False' and an error message.
- C) The tool runs an infinite loop.

Feedback: Option B is correct. Unsupported currencies trigger the boundary check.
